$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dL}{L} - \frac{dA}{A}$$

**Step 2: Express fractional area change in terms of radius and Poisson's ratio:**

$$A = \pi r^2 \Rightarrow \frac{dA}{A} = 2 \cdot \frac{dr}{r}$$

By Poisson's definition:

$$\nu = -\frac{dr / r}{dL / L} \Rightarrow \frac{dr}{r} = -\nu \cdot \frac{dL}{L}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{dA}{A} = 2 \cdot (-\nu) \cdot \frac{dL}{L} = -2\nu \cdot \frac{dL}{L}$$

**Step 3: Substitute back:**

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dL}{L} - (-2\nu) \cdot \frac{dL}{L}$$

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + (1 + 2\nu) \cdot \frac{dL}{L}$$

**Step 4: Divide both sides by strain $\varepsilon = dL / L$:**

$$\frac{dR / R}{\varepsilon} = (1 + 2\nu) + \frac{d\rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}$$

**Step 5: Identify the gauge factor:**

$$G = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon} = 1 + 2\nu + \frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}$$

This is the **master gauge factor equation** for the KTU board exam. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Wheatstone Bridge Output (Quarter-Bridge)

**Given:** A standard DC Wheatstone bridge with one active arm $R + \Delta R$ and three fixed arms $R$.

**Step 1: Write the two voltage divider outputs:**

$$V_A = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R}{R + R + \Delta R} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R}{2R + \Delta R}$$

$$V_B = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R}{R + R} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{1}{2}$$

**Step 2: Bridge output voltage:**

$$V_o = V_A - V_B = V_{ex} \cdot \left[ \frac{R}{2R + \Delta R} - \frac{1}{2} \right]$$

**Step 3: Combine over common denominator:**

$$V_o = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{2R - (2R + \Delta R)}{2(2R + \Delta R)} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{-\Delta R}{2(2R + \Delta R)}$$

**Step 4: Magnitude (ignore sign convention):**

$$|V_o| = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{4R + 2\Delta R}$$

**Step 5: Since $\Delta R \ll R$, approximate:**

$$V_o \approx \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}$$

**Step 6: Substitute $\Delta R / R = G \cdot \varepsilon$:**

$$V_o \approx \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon \quad \blacksquare$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Piezoelectric Voltage Equation

**Given:** Charge generated by a piezo crystal under stress:

$$Q = d \cdot F = d \cdot A \cdot \sigma$$

**Step 1: The voltage across a parallel-plate capacitor of the crystal:**

$$V = \frac{Q}{C}$$

**Step 2: Capacitance of the piezo element (parallel plate geometry):**

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon \cdot A}{t}$$

**Step 3: Substitute Q and C:**

$$V = \frac{d \cdot A \cdot \sigma}{(\varepsilon \cdot A) / t} = \frac{d \cdot t \cdot \sigma}{\varepsilon}$$

**Step 4: Define the voltage constant $g$:**

$$g = \frac{d}{\varepsilon}$$

**Step 5: Therefore:**

$$V = g \cdot t \cdot \sigma \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!TIP]
> This is a **favourite 7-mark derivation in KTU exams** — expect either this or the gauge factor derivation.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: KTU 2024 Numericals

#### Worked Example 1: Strain Gauge Output

**Problem:** A strain gauge with $G = 2.05$ and $R = 120 \ \Omega$ is bonded to a steel beam. The beam is loaded so that strain $\varepsilon = 1000 \ \mu\varepsilon$. The gauge is connected in a quarter-bridge with $V_{ex} = 5 \ \text{V}$. Find (a) $\Delta R$, (b) bridge output voltage.

**Solution:**

**(a) Fractional resistance change:**

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G \cdot \varepsilon = 2.05 \times 1000 \times 10^{-6} = 2.05 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\Delta R = 2.05 \times 10^{-3} \times 120 = 0.246 \ \Omega$$

**(b) Bridge output:**

$$V_o = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon = \frac{5}{4} \times 2.05 \times 10^{-3} = 2.5625 \ \text{mV}$$

✅ **Answer:** $\Delta R = 0.246 \ \Omega$, $V_o = 2.56 \ \text{mV}$

---

#### Worked Example 2: Gauge Factor from Material Constants

**Problem:** A constantan strain gauge has $\nu = 0.33$ and piezoresistive component $(\Delta \rho / \rho) / \varepsilon = 0.40$. Calculate the gauge factor.

**Solution:**

$$G = 1 + 2\nu + \frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon} = 1 + 2(0.33) + 0.40 = 1 + 0.66 + 0.40 = 2.06$$

✅ **Answer:** $G = 2.06$ (matches the standard constantan value)

---

#### Worked Example 3: Piezoelectric Output

**Problem:** A PZT-5A crystal has $d_{33} = 374 \ \text{pC/N}$, area $A = 1 \ \text{cm}^2$, thickness $t = 1 \ \text{mm}$, and is subjected to a force $F = 10 \ \text{N}$. Permittivity $\varepsilon = 1500 \varepsilon_0$. Find (a) generated charge, (b) output voltage.

**Solution:**

**(a) Charge:**

$$Q = d_{33} \cdot F = 374 \times 10^{-12} \times 10 = 3.74 \times 10^{-9} \ \text{C} = 3.74 \ \text{nC}$$

**(b) Voltage:**

Capacitance:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon A}{t} = \frac{1500 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 1 \times 10^{-4}}{1 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.328 \times 10^{-9} \ \text{F}$$

Voltage:

$$V = \frac{Q}{C} = \frac{3.74 \times 10^{-9}}{1.328 \times 10^{-9}} = 2.816 \ \text{V}$$

✅ **Answer:** $Q = 3.74 \ \text{nC}$, $V = 2.82 \ \text{V}$

---

#### Worked Example 4: Force from Piezo Output

**Problem:** A quartz crystal ($d = 2.3 \ \text{pC/N}$) produces $V = 0.5 \ \text{V}$ across a thickness $t = 2 \ \text{mm}$. Find the applied stress.

**Solution:**

$$V = g \cdot t \cdot \sigma \Rightarrow \sigma = \frac{V}{g \cdot t}$$

First, find $g$:

$$g = \frac{d}{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0} = \frac{2.3 \times 10^{-12}}{4.5 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12}} = 0.0577 \ \text{V·m/N}$$

Now:

$$\sigma = \frac{0.5}{0.0577 \times 2 \times 10^{-3}} = 4331 \ \text{Pa} = 4.33 \ \text{kPa}$$

✅ **Answer:** $\sigma = 4.33 \ \text{kPa}$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Strain Gauge Architecture & Operating Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Mechanical Force F applied to host structure] --> B[Strain epsilon induced in host]
    B --> C[Strain Gauge deforms with host]
    C --> D[Resistance R changes to R plus deltaR]
    D --> E[Wheatstone Bridge converts deltaR to voltage Vo]
    E --> F[Instrumentation Amplifier INA128]
    F --> G[ADC and Digital Readout]
    G --> H[Force / Pressure / Weight Display]

    subgraph SG_TYPE
        T1[Bonded Foil Gauge]
        T2[Semiconductor Gauge]
        T3[Thin Film MEMS Gauge]
    end
```

### 4.2 Wheatstone Bridge Topology with Strain Gauges

```mermaid
graph TD
    Vex[DC Excitation Vex] --> NodeTop
    NodeTop --> R1[R1]
    NodeTop --> R2[R2]
    R1 --> NodeA
    R2 --> NodeB
    NodeA --> R3[R3]
    NodeB --> R4[Active Gauge R plus deltaR]
    R3 --> GND1[Ground]
    R4 --> GND2[Ground]
    NodeA --> VoPos[Vo positive]
    NodeB --> VoNeg[Vo negative]
    VoPos --> DiffAmp[Differential Amplifier]
    VoNeg --> DiffAmp
    DiffAmp --> Out[Bridge Output Vo]
```

### 4.3 Piezoelectric Transducer Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MECH[Mechanical Stress sigma on crystal] --> CRYSTAL[Non-centrosymmetric Crystal e.g. Quartz PZT PVDF]
    CRYSTAL --> CHARGE[Surface Charge Q on electrodes]
    CHARGE --> CAP[Capacitance C of crystal stores charge]
    CAP --> VOLT[Voltage V equals Q over C]
    VOLT --> CHAMP[Charge Amplifier or FET Op-amp]
    CHAMP --> DSP[Signal Conditioning and Display]

    REV[Applied Voltage V] --> CRYSTAL2[Converse Piezoelectric Effect]
    CRYSTAL2 --> DISP[Mechanical Displacement x equals d times V]
```

### 4.4 Modes of Piezoelectric Operation (Block View)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Piezoelectric Modes] --> B[d33 Longitudinal]
    A --> C[d31 Transverse]
    A --> D[d15 Shear]
    B --> B1[Force parallel to polarization]
    B --> B2[Used in force sensors and inkjet]
    C --> C1[Force perpendicular to polarization]
    C --> C2[Used in accelerometers]
    D --> D1[Shear stress on polarized face]
    D --> D2[Used in high-frequency ultrasound]
```

### 4.5 Signal Conditioning Chain for Both Transducers

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SG[Strain Gauge in Bridge] --> IA1[Instrumentation Amplifier]
    PZ[Piezoelectric Sensor] --> CA[Charge Amplifier]
    IA1 --> FILT[Low-pass Filter]
    CA --> FILT
    FILT --> ADC[16-bit ADC]
    ADC --> MCU[Microcontroller or DSP]
    MCU --> DISP[LCD or PC Display]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters in KTU Viva:** Examiners often ask *"Why can't we directly connect a piezo sensor to an ADC?"* The answer is that piezo sensors have very high output impedance (in the GΩ range), and the charge will leak away. A **charge amplifier** (an op-amp with a feedback capacitor) converts the high-impedance charge into a low-impedance voltage signal.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
Define a strain gauge. What is gauge factor? Mention the gauge factor of a typical metallic strain gauge.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- A **strain gauge** is a passive resistive transducer that converts mechanical strain into a change in electrical resistance. (1 mark)
- **Gauge factor (G)** is defined as the ratio of fractional change in resistance to the applied strain:

$$G = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

- For a typical metallic foil gauge (e.g., constantan), **G ≈ 2.0** (1 mark).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sessional]**
What is the piezoelectric effect? Differentiate between direct and converse piezoelectric effects.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- The **piezoelectric effect** is the ability of certain non-centrosymmetric crystals to convert mechanical energy into electrical energy and vice versa. (1 mark)
- **Direct piezoelectric effect:** Mechanical stress → electric charge/voltage. (1 mark)
- **Converse piezoelectric effect:** Applied voltage → mechanical strain/displacement. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions with Internal Choice (ESE Pattern)

> Per KTU 2024 ESE guidelines, every Part B question carries 14 marks and offers an internal choice. Sub-parts (a) and (b) are typically 7 marks each.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Paper, Modified]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a strain gauge. Derive the expression for its gauge factor. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A strain gauge of resistance $120 \ \Omega$ and gauge factor 2.1 is bonded to a steel beam. It is connected in a quarter Wheatstone bridge with an excitation of 6 V. If the beam is subjected to a strain of $800 \ \mu\varepsilon$, calculate:
  (i) Change in resistance
  (ii) Bridge output voltage *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working Principle & Derivation (7 marks):**
- A strain gauge is a thin metallic foil (or wire) bonded to a flexible backing, which is then attached to the structure under test. (1 mark)
- When the structure deforms under load, the gauge deforms with it. The conductor's length increases and cross-section decreases, changing its resistance. (1 mark)
- This small $\Delta R$ is detected by a Wheatstone bridge, producing an output voltage proportional to strain. (1 mark)
- Derivation (from Section 3.1): Starting from $R = \rho L / A$, take differential, substitute Poisson's ratio, and obtain:

$$G = 1 + 2\nu + \frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon} \quad \text{(4 marks for derivation steps)}$$

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: $R = 120 \ \Omega$, $G = 2.1$, $V_{ex} = 6 \ \text{V}$, $\varepsilon = 800 \ \mu\varepsilon = 800 \times 10^{-6}$

**(i) Change in resistance:**

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G \cdot \varepsilon = 2.1 \times 800 \times 10^{-6} = 1.68 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\Delta R = 1.68 \times 10^{-3} \times 120 = 0.2016 \ \Omega \approx 0.202 \ \Omega \quad \text{(3 marks)}$$

**(ii) Bridge output voltage:**

$$V_o = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon = \frac{6}{4} \times 1.68 \times 10^{-3} = 2.52 \ \text{mV} \quad \text{(4 marks)}$$

✅ **Final Answer:** $\Delta R = 0.202 \ \Omega$, $V_o = 2.52 \ \text{mV}$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Main Paper, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the working principle of a piezoelectric transducer with a neat block diagram. List any four piezoelectric materials. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A PZT crystal has $d_{33} = 320 \ \text{pC/N}$, area $A = 2 \ \text{cm}^2$, and thickness $t = 0.5 \ \text{mm}$. A force of 20 N is applied. The relative permittivity of PZT is 1200. Calculate:
  (i) Charge generated
  (ii) Output voltage across the crystal *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Principle & Materials (7 marks):**
- A piezoelectric transducer uses non-centrosymmetric crystals that generate an electric charge when mechanically stressed (direct effect). (2 marks)
- Block diagram (as in Section 4.3) — (2 marks)
- Four common piezo materials: **Quartz, Rochelle salt, PZT (Lead Zirconate Titanate), PVDF (Polyvinylidene Fluoride)**. (1 mark)
- Brief mention of converse effect and the use of charge amplifier for high-impedance readout. (2 marks)

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: $d_{33} = 320 \ \text{pC/N} = 320 \times 10^{-12} \ \text{C/N}$, $A = 2 \ \text{cm}^2 = 2 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{m}^2$, $t = 0.5 \ \text{mm} = 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m}$, $F = 20 \ \text{N}$, $\varepsilon_r = 1200$.

**(i) Charge generated:**

$$Q = d_{33} \cdot F = 320 \times 10^{-12} \times 20 = 6.4 \times 10^{-9} \ \text{C} = 6.4 \ \text{nC} \quad \text{(3 marks)}$$

**(ii) Output voltage:**

Capacitance:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_r \cdot \varepsilon_0 \cdot A}{t} = \frac{1200 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 2 \times 10^{-4}}{0.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 4.25 \times 10^{-9} \ \text{F}$$

Voltage:

$$V = \frac{Q}{C} = \frac{6.4 \times 10^{-9}}{4.25 \times 10^{-9}} = 1.506 \ \text{V} \approx 1.51 \ \text{V} \quad \text{(4 marks)}$$

✅ **Final Answer:** $Q = 6.4 \ \text{nC}$, $V = 1.51 \ \text{V}$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks (KTU Board Pattern):**
> 1. **Unit Confusion (–2 marks):** Students often forget to convert $\mu\varepsilon$ to $\varepsilon$ (i.e., multiply by $10^{-6}$). A $1000 \ \mu\varepsilon$ strain is $10^{-3}$, not $1000$.
> 2. **Missing Factor of 4 in Wheatstone Bridge (–2 marks):** Many students write $V_o = V_{ex} \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon$ for a *quarter-bridge* — that is the *full-bridge* formula. For quarter-bridge, it is $V_o = (V_{ex} / 4) \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon$.
> 3. **Confusing $d$ and $g$ constants (–1 mark):** $d$ relates charge to force (units C/N); $g$ relates voltage to stress (units V·m/N). Don't mix them up.
> 4. **Not stating the piezoelectric material:** Examiners deduct marks if you just write "crystal" — always name it (quartz, PZT, etc.).
> 5. **Forgetting the charge amplifier:** In piezo questions, mentioning that a *charge amplifier is required due to high output impedance* is worth 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Sign error in $\Delta R$:** When the bridge has the active gauge in the *opposite* arm, the sign of $V_o$ flips. State assumptions clearly.

---

### 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **The Last-Minute Revision Sheet (Pin This on Your Wall!):**

- ✅ A **strain gauge** is a **passive** resistive transducer; **piezoelectric** is an **active** transducer (generates its own voltage).
- ✅ Master strain gauge equation: $\Delta R / R = G \cdot \varepsilon$ where $G$ is the **gauge factor**.
- ✅ Gauge factor formula: $G = 1 + 2\nu + (\Delta \rho / \rho) / \varepsilon$.
- ✅ For constantan: $G \approx 2.0$; for semiconductors: $G \approx 100$–$200$.
- ✅ Wheatstone bridge output: $V_o = (V_{ex} / 4) \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon$ (quarter-bridge).
- ✅ Full-bridge: $V_o = V_{ex} \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon$ (4× more sensitive).
- ✅ Strain gauge types: unbonded, bonded foil, semiconductor, thin-film, MEMS.
- ✅ Piezoelectric effect: discovered by **Curie brothers in 1880**.
- ✅ Direct effect: stress → charge, $Q = d \cdot F$.
- ✅ Converse effect: voltage → displacement, $x = d \cdot V$.
- ✅ Piezo voltage equation: $V = g \cdot t \cdot \sigma$.
- ✅ Relation: $d = g \cdot \varepsilon = g \cdot \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0$.
- ✅ Common piezo materials: quartz, PZT, PVDF, Rochelle salt, tourmaline.
- ✅ Modes: d₃₃ (longitudinal), d₃₁ (transverse), d₁₅ (shear).
- ✅ Piezo sensors need **charge amplifiers** due to very high output impedance.
- ✅ Biomedical applications: pulse sensor, blood pressure, ultrasound, prosthetic load, accelerometer.
- ✅ Key constants to remember: $G_{metallic} \approx 2.0$, $G_{semi} \approx 100$–$200$, $d_{33}(PZT) \approx 374 \ \text{pC/N}$, $d_{33}(quartz) \approx 2.3 \ \text{pC/N}$.
- ✅ Units drill: $\Delta R$ in Ω, $G$ dimensionless, $\varepsilon$ dimensionless (or $\mu\varepsilon$), $Q$ in C, $V$ in V, $d$ in C/N, $g$ in V·m/N.
- ✅ **Biomedical application map:** Strain gauge → electronic weighing scale, prosthetic force; Piezo → fetal heart sound sensor (phonocardiograph), ultrasound probe, arterial tonometer, MEMS accelerometer in wearables.
- ✅ Common KTU formula trap: Forgetting the factor of 4 in the bridge equation.
- ✅ Always **state the assumption** that $\Delta R \ll R$ when simplifying the bridge output equation.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Mechanical Transducers (Strain Gauges & Piezoelectric)**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020 outcomes, and revised Bloom's Taxonomy (Apply / Analyse / Evaluate levels).*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermal Transducers: thermocouples, thermistors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBMT404 - BIOSENSORS & TRANSDUCERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Thermal Transducers: thermocouples, thermistors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Thermal Transducer?

A **Thermal Transducer** is a primary sensing device that converts a physical quantity—most commonly **temperature**—into a measurable electrical signal (voltage, current, or resistance change). In the context of biomedical engineering (KTU 2024 Scheme), thermal transducers form the front-end of every clinical thermometer, incubator feedback loop, calorimeter, skin-temperature wearable, and PCR thermal cycler.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *A thermal transducer is a device that exploits predictable temperature-dependent variations in electrical properties (EMF, resistance, or current) of a material to provide a quantifiable output proportional to thermal energy.*

Two of the most important families studied in Module 3 are:
1. **Thermocouples** — based on the *Seebeck Effect* (EMF generation).
2. **Thermistors** — based on *semiconductor resistance change* with temperature.

---

### 1.2 Thermocouple — Formal Definition

A **Thermocouple** is a temperature-sensing element consisting of **two dissimilar conductors (thermoelements) joined at one end (the measuring or "hot" junction)**, with the other two ends kept at a reference temperature (the "cold" or reference junction). A thermoelectric EMF is generated that is a function of the temperature difference between the two junctions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Fact for Exams:** A thermocouple does **not** measure absolute temperature; it measures the **temperature *difference*** between the hot and cold junctions. The cold junction must therefore be compensated for accurate absolute readings.

#### Conceptual Analogy — The "Two Friends on a See-Saw" 🛝

Imagine two friends sitting on a see-saw, but the see-saw is made of two *different* metals (say, copper on the left, constantan on the right). When the middle pivot (the hot junction) gets hot, one metal's electrons get more energetic than the other's. Because each metal "pushes" electrons with a different strength at the same temperature, the imbalance creates a small voltage at the open ends — just like the see-saw tilting when one side gets heavier. The further the hot junction is from the reference (cold) end, the bigger the tilt (voltage). This imbalance is the **Seebeck EMF**.

---

### 1.3 Thermistor — Formal Definition

A **Thermistor** (thermal resistor) is a passive semiconductor device whose electrical resistance is a **strong, predictable, and non-linear function of temperature**. Unlike a metal RTD (where resistance *increases* linearly with temperature), thermistors are fabricated from metal-oxide ceramics (e.g., Mn, Ni, Co, Fe oxides) and exhibit a much larger temperature coefficient—typically **−3% to −6% per °C** for NTC types.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Classification of Thermistors:**
> - **NTC (Negative Temperature Coefficient):** Resistance *decreases* as temperature rises. Most common.
> - **PTC (Positive Temperature Coefficient):** Resistance *increases* sharply after a switching point (used as resettable fuses).
> - **CTR (Critical Temperature Resistor):** Resistance changes abruptly at a critical temperature (switching applications).

#### Conceptual Analogy — The "Crowded Tunnel" 🚇

Picture a tunnel filled with commuters (electrons in a semiconductor). As the weather gets hotter, the commuters (charge carriers) get more energetic and more of them enter the tunnel from the doped ceramic — so it's *easier* for current to flow. The "narrowness" (resistance) therefore *decreases* with temperature. This is exactly the **NTC behavior**.

---

### 1.4 Standard Metrics and Constants (Bold for Emphasis)

- **Seebeck Coefficient (S):** typically **5 µV/°C to 50 µV/°C** depending on the thermocouple type.
- **Standard Reference Temperature:** **0 °C (273.15 K)** — the ice-point used in thermocouple calibration tables (NIST ITS-90).
- **Thermistor Beta Constant (β):** typically **3000 K to 5000 K** for common NTC thermistors.
- **Standard NTC Resistance:** **10 kΩ at 25 °C** is the most commercially common rating.

> [!NOTE]
> The **Seebeck coefficient** is *not* a true constant — it is mildly temperature-dependent, which is why high-precision thermocouple tables are tabulated at fine temperature intervals (every 1 °C).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Thermoelectric Effects (Foundation of Thermocouple Theory)

A thermocouple is governed by **three interrelated thermoelectric phenomena** — examiners frequently ask students to differentiate between them.

#### (a) Seebeck Effect (Discovered by Thomas Seebeck, 1821)
- **What:** A temperature gradient across a junction of two dissimilar conductors generates an EMF.
- **How:** Charge carriers (electrons/holes) diffuse from the hot side to the cold side at different rates in the two materials, creating a net potential difference.
- **Equation:**
  
$$E = \int_{T_{ref}}^{T_{hot}} \left[ S_B(T) - S_A(T) \right] \, dT$$

  where $S_A$ and $S_B$ are the Seebeck coefficients of materials A and B.

- **Why it matters:** This is the *only* effect that *generates* EMF — the operating principle of every thermocouple.

#### (b) Peltier Effect (Discovered by Jean Peltier, 1834) — Inverse of Seebeck
- **What:** When a current is forced through a junction of two dissimilar metals, heat is either *absorbed* or *released* at the junction, depending on current direction.
- **How:** Carriers transport energy across the junction; they either deliver energy (cooling) or absorb energy (heating).
- **Equation:** Heat absorbed per unit time:
  
$$\dot{Q} = \Pi_{AB} \cdot I$$

  where $\Pi_{AB}$ is the Peltier coefficient and $I$ is the current.

- **Why it matters:** Used in **thermoelectric coolers (Peltier modules)** — actively used in PCR thermal cyclers in biomedical labs.

#### (c) Thomson Effect (Predicted by William Thomson / Lord Kelvin, 1854)
- **What:** A single conductor carrying current while subjected to a temperature gradient either absorbs or evolves heat along its length.
- **How:** Even in a *single* homogeneous wire, a temperature gradient combined with current causes reversible heating/cooling.
- **Equation:**
  
$$\dot{q} = -\sigma \cdot \vec{J} \cdot \frac{dT}{dx}$$

  where $\sigma$ is the Thomson coefficient (units: V/K).

- **Why it matters:** Required correction term in high-precision thermocouple metrology and in the **Kelvin Relations** linking all three coefficients.

---

### 2.2 Fundamental Laws of Thermocouples (High-Yield for KTU 14-Mark Questions)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These three laws form the *theoretical backbone* of all thermocouple-based numerical problems. Memorize them rigorously.

**Law 1 — Law of Intermediate Metals:**
> *Inserting a third metal C into the thermocouple circuit does not alter the net EMF, provided both ends of metal C are at the same temperature.*

This is why we can introduce **copper extension wires, terminal blocks, and measuring instruments** into the loop without corrupting the measurement.

**Law 2 — Law of Intermediate Temperatures:**
> *The EMF generated by a thermocouple with junctions at $T_1$ and $T_3$ equals the sum of EMFs produced with junctions at $T_1$–$T_2$ and $T_2$–$T_3$.*

Mathematically:

$$E_{T_1 \to T_3} = E_{T_1 \to T_2} + E_{T_2 \to T_3}$$

**Law 3 — Law of Additive / Substitution EMF:**
> *For a given pair of junctions at temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$, the EMF depends only on the two materials and the temperature difference — not on the wire lengths, diameters, or geometry.*

---

### 2.3 Cold Junction Compensation (CJC) — Compensating the Reference

Since a thermocouple outputs $E(T_{hot}, T_{ref})$ but most applications need $T_{hot}$ in absolute terms, the reference junction must be compensated. The two industry-standard methods are:

1. **Hardware (Ice-Bath) CJC:** Physically maintain the cold junction at 0 °C in a stirred ice slurry. Reference NIST tables directly.
2. **Software (Electronic) CJC:** Use a separate precision sensor (e.g., a silicon diode or RTD) to measure $T_{ref}$, then *add* the equivalent thermoelectric voltage $E(T_{ref}, 0\,°C)$ to the measured EMF using lookup tables.

> [!NOTE]
> Modern KTU syllabus context: Almost all clinical and industrial instruments use **software CJC** because ice baths are impractical in field-deployable biomedical wearables.

---

### 2.4 Thermistor Theory — The Beta (β) Equation and Steinhart–Hart Model

The resistance–temperature characteristic of an NTC thermistor is governed by two principal models:

**Model 1 — Simple Beta Equation (One-Point Calibration Required):**

$$R_T = R_0 \cdot \exp\left[ \beta \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right) \right]$$

- $R_T$ = resistance at absolute temperature $T$ (in Kelvin).
- $R_0$ = resistance at reference temperature $T_0$ (commonly 298.15 K = 25 °C).
- $\beta$ = characteristic temperature constant of the thermistor material (in Kelvin).

**Model 2 — Steinhart–Hart Equation (Three-Point Calibration, High Accuracy):**

$$\frac{1}{T} = A + B \ln(R_T) + C \left[ \ln(R_T) \right]^3$$

- $A$, $B$, $C$ = empirically determined coefficients (vendor-calibrated).

**Temperature Coefficient of Resistance (TCR), α:**

$$\alpha = \frac{1}{R_T} \cdot \frac{dR_T}{dT} = -\frac{\beta}{T^2}$$

For a typical NTC with $\beta = 4000$ K at $T = 300$ K: $\alpha \approx -0.0444 / \text{K}$ = **−4.44% per °C**, which is roughly **10× larger** than a platinum RTD.

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the following table — it covers 80% of KTU Module 3 numerical questions on thermal transducers.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Constants |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | Seebeck EMF (integral form) | $E = \int_{T_{ref}}^{T_{hot}} (S_B - S_A)\, dT$ | Volts (V) |
| 2 | Approximate Seebeck EMF | $E \approx S_{AB}\,(T_{hot} - T_{ref})$ | S in V/°C |
| 3 | Peltier Heat Rate | $\dot{Q} = \Pi_{AB} \cdot I$ | Watts (W) |
| 4 | Thomson Heat Density | $\dot{q} = -\sigma \, J \, \dfrac{dT}{dx}$ | W/m |
| 5 | Law of Intermediate Temperatures | $E_{13} = E_{12} + E_{23}$ | Volts |
| 6 | Thermistor Beta Equation | $R_T = R_0 \exp\!\left[ \beta\!\left(\tfrac{1}{T} - \tfrac{1}{T_0}\right) \right]$ | Ω, K |
| 7 | Temperature Coefficient (TCR) | $\alpha = -\dfrac{\beta}{T^2}$ | per Kelvin |
| 8 | Steinhart–Hart | $\tfrac{1}{T} = A + B\ln R + C(\ln R)^3$ | K |
| 9 | Sensitivity (Thermocouple) | $S = dE/dT$ | µV/°C |
| 10 | Sensitivity (Thermistor) | $S_R = dR/dT = -\beta R / T^2$ | Ω/K |

---

### 2.6 Real-World Biomedical Engineering Applications

- **Clinical Thermometry:** T-type (Cu–Constantan) thermocouples in esophageal/rectal probes because of bio-compatibility and low Seebeck drift.
- **Neonatal Incubators:** NTC thermistor arrays (10 kΩ at 25 °C) provide skin-temperature feedback with 0.01 °C resolution.
- **PCR Thermal Cyclers:** Peltier-effect modules heat/cool bio-reagent blocks at >4 °C/s.
- **Hyperthermia / Cryosurgery Probes:** Multi-point thermocouple arrays monitor tissue temperature in real time.
- **Calorimeters (Metabolic Studies):** NTC thermistors measure minute heat-flow changes in respiration calorimetry.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Seebeck EMF from Carrier Diffusion

Consider two dissimilar metals A and B joined at the hot junction (temperature $T_H$) and open at the cold junction (temperature $T_C$). Electrons in the hotter end gain kinetic energy and diffuse toward the cold end.

**Step 1:** The number of electrons leaving the hot end in material A (per unit area, per unit time) by the *Drickamer–Holloman* model is:

$$n_{diff} = n \cdot v_{th} \cdot e^{-E_f / kT}$$

**Step 2:** The corresponding net flux in material A is proportional to the temperature gradient:

$$J_A = -D_A \cdot \frac{dn_A}{dx} - \mu_A \cdot n_A \cdot \frac{dE}{dx}$$

where $D_A$ is the diffusion coefficient and $\mu_A$ is mobility.

**Step 3:** In steady state, current continuity requires the gradient of the electrochemical potential to balance diffusion. The resulting electric field in A is:

$$\mathcal{E}_A = -\frac{k}{q}\left(\frac{1}{\sigma_A}\frac{d\sigma_A}{dT}\right)\frac{dT}{dx}$$

**Step 4:** Integrating across the length of wire A from cold to hot junction:

$$V_A = -\int_{T_C}^{T_H} \frac{k}{q} \cdot \frac{d \ln \sigma_A}{dT} \, dT = -\int_{T_C}^{T_H} S_A(T)\, dT$$

**Step 5:** Repeating for material B and applying superposition:

$$E_{total} = V_A - V_B = \int_{T_C}^{T_H} \left[ S_B(T) - S_A(T) \right] dT$$

**Step 6:** For first-order approximation (small $\Delta T$ where $S$ is nearly constant):

$$E_{total} \approx S_{AB} \cdot (T_H - T_C) \quad \text{where } S_{AB} = S_B - S_A$$

This is the **fundamental Seebeck equation** quoted in 90% of KTU derivations.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Thermistor Beta Equation from Semiconductor Physics

We model the NTC thermistor as an intrinsic semiconductor whose carrier concentration follows Maxwell–Boltzmann statistics.

**Step 1:** Intrinsic carrier concentration:

$$n_i \propto T^{3/2} \cdot \exp\!\left( -\frac{E_g}{2kT} \right)$$

where $E_g$ is the band-gap energy.

**Step 2:** Conductivity $\sigma$ is proportional to carrier concentration:

$$\sigma = q \cdot n_i \cdot (\mu_e + \mu_h) \propto T^{3/2} \cdot \exp\!\left( -\frac{E_g}{2kT} \right)$$

**Step 3:** Resistance is inversely proportional to conductivity:

$$R_T \propto \frac{1}{\sigma} \propto T^{-3/2} \cdot \exp\!\left( \frac{E_g}{2kT} \right)$$

**Step 4:** Define $\beta \equiv E_g / 2k$ (a material constant in Kelvin). Then:

$$R_T = C \cdot T^{-3/2} \cdot \exp\!\left( \frac{\beta}{T} \right)$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate at reference temperature $T_0$ (e.g., 298.15 K):

$$R_0 = C \cdot T_0^{-3/2} \cdot \exp\!\left( \frac{\beta}{T_0} \right)$$

**Step 6:** Divide $R_T$ by $R_0$:

$$\frac{R_T}{R_0} = \left(\frac{T_0}{T}\right)^{3/2} \cdot \exp\!\left[ \beta\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right) \right]$$

**Step 7:** Over a narrow range (e.g., 0–100 °C), the prefactor $(T_0/T)^{3/2}$ varies by less than 3%, so it is absorbed into a modified constant $R_0'$:

$$\boxed{R_T = R_0' \cdot \exp\!\left[ \beta\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right) \right]}$$

This is the **standard beta equation** used in every KTU thermistor problem.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Sensitivity of Thermistor (TCR)

Differentiate the beta equation with respect to $T$:

**Step 1:** Take natural log of $R_T = R_0' \exp\!\left[ \beta\left(\tfrac{1}{T} - \tfrac{1}{T_0}\right) \right]$:

$$\ln R_T = \ln R_0' + \beta\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate both sides w.r.t. $T$:

$$\frac{1}{R_T}\frac{dR_T}{dT} = -\frac{\beta}{T^2}$$

**Step 3:** Therefore TCR $\alpha$ is:

$$\alpha = -\frac{\beta}{T^2}$$

**Step 4:** Numerical check at $T = 300$ K, $\beta = 4000$ K:

$$\alpha = -\frac{4000}{(300)^2} = -\frac{4000}{90000} = -0.04444 \text{ K}^{-1}$$

So the resistance changes by **~4.44% per °C** at room temperature.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Law of Intermediate Temperatures

Consider a thermocouple AB with junctions at $T_1$ and $T_3$.

**Step 1:** Total EMF:

$$E_{AB}(T_1, T_3) = \int_{T_1}^{T_3} \left[ S_B(T) - S_A(T) \right] dT$$

**Step 2:** Introduce intermediate temperature $T_2$ where $T_1 < T_2 < T_3$:

$$E_{AB}(T_1, T_3) = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} (S_B - S_A) dT + \int_{T_2}^{T_3} (S_B - S_A) dT$$

**Step 3:** Recognize the two integrals as EMFs for the sub-couples:

$$E_{AB}(T_1, T_3) = E_{AB}(T_1, T_2) + E_{AB}(T_2, T_3)$$

This is the **Law of Intermediate Temperatures** — a critical KTU 14-mark derivation.

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A K-type thermocouple has an average Seebeck coefficient of $S = 41 \text{ µV/°C}$. The hot junction is at 220 °C and the cold junction is at 25 °C. Find (a) the generated EMF and (b) the temperature if the EMF is 7.38 mV with cold junction at 25 °C.

**Solution (a):**

$$E = S \cdot (T_H - T_C) = 41 \times 10^{-6} \times (220 - 25)$$

$$E = 41 \times 10^{-6} \times 195 = 7.995 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V} = 7.995 \text{ mV}$$

**Solution (b):**

$$T_H = T_C + \frac{E}{S} = 25 + \frac{7.38 \times 10^{-3}}{41 \times 10^{-6}} = 25 + 180 = 205\,°C$$

---

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example — Thermistor

> **Problem:** An NTC thermistor has $R_0 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$ at $T_0 = 298.15$ K and $\beta = 3950$ K. Find the resistance at body temperature (37 °C = 310.15 K).

**Solution:**

$$R_T = 10{,}000 \cdot \exp\!\left[ 3950 \left( \frac{1}{310.15} - \frac{1}{298.15} \right) \right]$$

**Step 1:** Compute the reciprocal differences:

$$\frac{1}{310.15} = 3.2242 \times 10^{-3} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

$$\frac{1}{298.15} = 3.3540 \times 10^{-3} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

$$\Delta = -1.298 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

**Step 2:** Multiply by β:

$$\beta \cdot \Delta = 3950 \times (-1.298 \times 10^{-4}) = -0.5128$$

**Step 3:** Apply the exponential:

$$R_T = 10{,}000 \cdot e^{-0.5128} = 10{,}000 \times 0.5988 \approx 5988 \text{ }\Omega$$

$$\boxed{R_T \approx 5.99 \text{ k}\Omega}$$

This is realistic — a typical 10 kΩ NTC drops to about 6 kΩ at body temperature.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Architecture — Thermocouple Measurement Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hot Junction T_H] --> B[Thermoelement A]
    A --> C[Thermoelement B]
    B --> D[Cold Junction T_C]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Copper Lead Wires]
    E --> F[Amplifier and Linearizer]
    F --> G[ADC]
    G --> H[Microcontroller with CJC Table]
    H --> I[Display Temperature in Degree Celsius]
    D -.Reference Sensor.-> J[RTD or Silicon Diode for CJC]
    J --> H
```

This flowchart maps the **functional flow of a software-compensated thermocouple instrument** — the standard KTU 2024 architecture for clinical and industrial systems.

---

### 4.2 Block Architecture — Thermistor Signal Conditioning Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Body or Environment Heat Source] --> P2[NTC Thermistor Bead]
    P2 --> P3[Resistance Change delta R]
    P3 --> P4[Voltage Divider or Wheatstone Bridge]
    P4 --> P5[Instrumentation Amplifier]
    P5 --> P6[ADC with Look Up Table]
    P6 --> P7[Microcontroller]
    P7 --> P8[Linearized Temperature Output]
    P2 -.Self Heating.-> P9[Decide Drive Current Below 1 mA]
```

The **self-heating warning** at P9 is critical: thermistors are tiny ceramic beads; even 1 mA can raise their temperature by 0.5–1 °C and corrupt the reading.

---

### 4.3 Thermistor Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Thermistor Family] --> B1[NTC Negative Temperature Coefficient]
    A0 --> B2[PTC Positive Temperature Coefficient]
    A0 --> B3[CTR Critical Temperature Resistor]
    B1 --> C1[Oxide Ceramics Mn Ni Co Fe]
    B1 --> C2[Used in Inlet Temperature Sensing]
    B2 --> D1[BaTiO3 Doped with Rare Earths]
    B2 --> D2[Used as Resettable Fuse]
    B3 --> E1[Vanadium Oxide Based]
    B3 --> E2[Used in Motor Protection Relays]
```

---

### 4.4 Comparison — Thermocouple vs Thermistor vs RTD

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1[Choose Temperature Sensor] --> Q2{Need Wide Range above 600 Degree Celsius}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3[Use Type K or Type S Thermocouple]
    Q2 -- No --> Q4{Need High Accuracy and Linearity}
    Q4 -- Yes --> Q5[Use Platinum RTD PT100 or PT1000]
    Q4 -- No --> Q6{Need High Sensitivity in Narrow Range}
    Q6 -- Yes --> Q7[Use NTC Thermistor with Beta Equation]
    Q6 -- No --> Q8[Re Evaluate Application]
```

This decision tree is **exactly the kind of analytical diagram** KTU expects in 14-mark design questions.

---

### 4.5 Cold-Junction Compensation Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Thermocouple EMF E measured] --> M2[Look Up NIST Table for T ref]
    M3[Reference Sensor Reads T ref] --> M2
    M2 --> M4[Add EMF Equivalent of T ref minus 0 Degree Celsius]
    M4 --> M5[Add to Measured E]
    M5 --> M6[Look Up Final E to get Absolute T hot]
    M6 --> M7[Output T hot in Degree Celsius]
```

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners expect students to identify **Law of Intermediate Temperatures** as the theoretical justification for the CJC addition step in M4–M5.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]
**Define the Seebeck effect. How is it different from the Peltier effect?**

**Model Answer:**
The **Seebeck effect** is the phenomenon in which a temperature difference between two junctions of dissimilar conductors produces an electromotive force (EMF). It is the underlying principle of the thermocouple. Mathematically, $E = S_{AB}\,\Delta T$ where $S_{AB}$ is the relative Seebeck coefficient.

The **Peltier effect** is the converse: when a current is forced through a junction of two dissimilar metals, heat is either absorbed or evolved at the junction. It is used in active coolers/heaters (Peltier modules), not in temperature sensing. Quantitatively, $\dot{Q} = \Pi_{AB} I$.

**Key Difference Table:**

| Aspect | Seebeck | Peltier |
|---|---|---|
| Cause | Temperature gradient | Forced current |
| Effect | Generates EMF | Generates/ absorbs heat |
| Used in | Thermocouples (sensors) | Peltier coolers (actuators) |

(3 marks)

---

#### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is a thermistor? Differentiate between NTC and PTC types.**

**Model Answer:**
A **thermistor** (thermal resistor) is a temperature-sensitive semiconductor whose resistance changes predictably with temperature. It is made from sintered metal oxides (Mn, Ni, Co, Fe).

- **NTC (Negative Temperature Coefficient):** Resistance *decreases* with rising temperature. Used for temperature measurement, inrush current limiting, and biomedical sensing. Conduction increases because more charge carriers are thermally excited across the band gap.
- **PTC (Positive Temperature Coefficient):** Resistance *increases* sharply above a switching temperature. Used as resettable fuses and motor protection. The mechanism is a phase change in doped barium titanate.

(3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### 🔹 Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — July 2024]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working principle of a thermocouple. Define the Seebeck coefficient. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Type K thermocouple is used to measure the temperature of a furnace. The hot junction is at 800 °C and the cold junction is at 30 °C. The average Seebeck coefficient for Type K is 41 µV/°C. Determine: (i) the open-circuit EMF generated, (ii) the hot-junction temperature when the measured EMF is 31.5 mV with the same cold-junction temperature. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

A **thermocouple** consists of two dissimilar metal wires (e.g., Chromel and Alumel for Type K) welded together at one end to form the **measuring (hot) junction**. The other two ends are connected to a measuring instrument and form the **reference (cold) junction**.

**Working Principle:** When the hot and cold junctions are at different temperatures, charge carriers diffuse from the hot to the cold end at different rates in the two metals, producing a net **thermoelectric EMF** (Seebeck effect). The magnitude of this EMF depends only on the two materials and the temperature difference.

**Seebeck Coefficient Definition:** The Seebeck coefficient $S_{AB}$ of a thermocouple pair (A, B) is the EMF generated per unit temperature difference:

$$S_{AB} = \lim_{\Delta T \to 0} \frac{\Delta E}{\Delta T} = \frac{dE}{dT}$$

Its units are **V/K** (typically quoted in µV/°C). For Type K, $S \approx 41$ µV/°C near room temperature.

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H1[Hot Junction T_H] --> H2[Wire A Chromel]
    H1 --> H3[Wire B Alumel]
    H2 --> H4[Measuring Instrument with Cold Junction]
    H3 --> H4
    H4 --> H5[EMF Output in mV]
```

(7 marks — for diagram 2, principle 3, definition 2)

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:**
- $T_H = 800$ °C, $T_C = 30$ °C, $S = 41$ µV/°C.
- New measured $E = 31.5$ mV with $T_C = 30$ °C (same as before).

**(i) Open-circuit EMF:**

$$E = S \cdot (T_H - T_C) = 41 \times 10^{-6} \times (800 - 30)$$

$$E = 41 \times 10^{-6} \times 770 = 31{,}570 \times 10^{-6} = 31.57 \text{ mV}$$

$$\boxed{E \approx 31.57 \text{ mV}}$$

**(ii) New hot-junction temperature when $E = 31.5$ mV:**

$$T_H = T_C + \frac{E}{S} = 30 + \frac{31.5 \times 10^{-3}}{41 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$T_H = 30 + \frac{31{,}500}{41} = 30 + 768.29$$

$$\boxed{T_H \approx 798.29\,°C \approx 798\,°C}$$

(7 marks — each sub-part ~3.5 marks)

---

#### 🔹 Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive the beta equation for an NTC thermistor starting from semiconductor carrier statistics. Discuss its limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An NTC thermistor has a resistance of 10 kΩ at 25 °C and a beta value of 3950 K. Calculate its resistance at: (i) 0 °C, (ii) 50 °C, and (iii) 100 °C. Comment on the non-linearity of the response. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

Refer to **Derivation 2 in Section 3.2** above for the full step-by-step.

**Summary of Derivation:**
1. Start with intrinsic carrier concentration: $n_i \propto T^{3/2} \exp(-E_g / 2kT)$.
2. Conductivity $\sigma \propto n_i$.
3. Resistance $R \propto 1/\sigma \propto T^{-3/2} \exp(E_g / 2kT)$.
4. Define $\beta = E_g / 2k$, ratio of resistances at $T$ and $T_0$, and absorb the weak $(T_0/T)^{3/2}$ factor into a modified $R_0'$ to obtain:

$$R_T = R_0' \cdot \exp\!\left[ \beta \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right) \right]$$

**Limitations of the Beta Equation:**
- Valid only over a *narrow* temperature range (typically < 100 °C span).
- Assumes $\beta$ is constant — in reality, $\beta$ drifts with temperature.
- Less accurate than the **Steinhart–Hart** three-coefficient model.
- Ignores self-heating effects at high measurement currents.

(7 marks — derivation 5, limitations 2)

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:** $R_0 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$ at $T_0 = 298.15$ K (25 °C), $\beta = 3950$ K.

**General formula:** $R_T = 10{,}000 \cdot \exp\!\left[ 3950 \left( \tfrac{1}{T} - \tfrac{1}{298.15} \right) \right]$

**(i) At $T = 273.15$ K (0 °C):**

$$\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} = \frac{1}{273.15} - \frac{1}{298.15} = 3.6610 \times 10^{-3} - 3.3540 \times 10^{-3} = 3.069 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

$$3950 \times 3.069 \times 10^{-4} = 1.2124$$

$$R_T = 10{,}000 \cdot e^{1.2124} = 10{,}000 \times 3.3626 = 33{,}626 \text{ }\Omega$$

$$\boxed{R_{0\,°C} \approx 33.63 \text{ k}\Omega}$$

**(ii) At $T = 323.15$ K (50 °C):**

$$\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} = \frac{1}{323.15} - \frac{1}{298.15} = 3.0945 \times 10^{-3} - 3.3540 \times 10^{-3} = -2.595 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

$$3950 \times (-2.595 \times 10^{-4}) = -1.0251$$

$$R_T = 10{,}000 \cdot e^{-1.0251} = 10{,}000 \times 0.3588 = 3588 \text{ }\Omega$$

$$\boxed{R_{50\,°C} \approx 3.59 \text{ k}\Omega}$$

**(iii) At $T = 373.15$ K (100 °C):**

$$\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} = \frac{1}{373.15} - \frac{1}{298.15} = 2.6800 \times 10^{-3} - 3.3540 \times 10^{-3} = -6.740 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

$$3950 \times (-6.740 \times 10^{-4}) = -2.6623$$

$$R_T = 10{,}000 \cdot e^{-2.6623} = 10{,}000 \times 0.0699 = 699 \text{ }\Omega$$

$$\boxed{R_{100\,°C} \approx 0.70 \text{ k}\Omega}$$

**Comment on Non-Linearity:**
- 0–25 °C span (25 °C): ΔR = 33.63 − 10 = 23.63 kΩ → **0.945 kΩ/°C**
- 25–50 °C span (25 °C): ΔR = 10 − 3.59 = 6.41 kΩ → **0.256 kΩ/°C**
- 50–100 °C span (50 °C): ΔR = 3.59 − 0.70 = 2.89 kΩ → **0.0578 kΩ/°C**

The sensitivity **drops by an order of magnitude** over the 0–100 °C range, confirming strong **non-linearity** of the exponential R–T characteristic. This is the principal reason for using Steinhart–Hart or lookup tables in precision thermistor instrumentation.

(7 marks — each sub-part ~2, comment 1)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Typically Lose Marks in KTU Module 3 Thermal Transducer Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert °C to Kelvin** before plugging into the beta equation. The formula **only** works with absolute temperature.
> 2. **Confusing Seebeck and Peltier effects** — examiners frequently set trick questions asking *which effect is used in a thermocouple?* (Answer: Seebeck, NOT Peltier. Peltier is the *inverse* and is used in coolers.)
> 3. **Misapplying the Law of Intermediate Metals** — students forget that the *two* ends of the inserted third metal must be at the *same* temperature for the law to hold.
> 4. **Ignoring self-heating in thermistors** — using a large excitation current (>1 mA through a small bead) can cause the thermistor to heat itself by 1–2 °C, corrupting the reading. Always state the drive current.
> 5. **Treating $\beta$ as a true constant** — it drifts slowly with temperature; for high-precision work, the Steinhart–Hart equation is required.
> 6. **Sign errors in the Seebeck integral** — the EMF is $S_B - S_A$ integrated, not the absolute sum; wrong sign conventions cost full marks in 14-mark derivations.
> 7. **Unit confusion in TCR** — $\alpha$ has units of K⁻¹ (or %/°C × 0.01), not ppm/°C unless explicitly stated.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3 Thermal Transducers**

- ☐ A **thermal transducer** converts temperature into a measurable electrical quantity (EMF, resistance, current).
- ☐ A **thermocouple** uses the **Seebeck effect** — two dissimilar metals joined at the hot junction, with the EMF being a function of $T_{hot} - T_{cold}$.
- ☐ **Seebeck coefficient $S_{AB}$** = EMF per °C, units µV/°C, typical range 5–50 µV/°C.
- ☐ **Peltier effect** = inverse of Seebeck; current-driven heat pumping (used in PCR cyclers, NOT in sensors).
- ☐ **Thomson effect** = heat evolution in a single conductor with current and temperature gradient.
- ☐ **Law of Intermediate Metals** — a third metal with both ends at the same temperature does not affect EMF.
- ☐ **Law of Intermediate Temperatures** — $E_{13} = E_{12} + E_{23}$; foundation of cold-junction compensation.
- ☐ **Standard thermocouple types** — J (Fe–CuNi), K (Chromel–Alumel, most common), T (Cu–CuNi, bio-safe), E (high sensitivity), R/S (Pt–Rh, high-temperature), B (high-temp, no cold-junc correction needed).
- ☐ **Cold-Junction Compensation (CJC)** — must be performed because thermocouples measure *differences*, not absolute temperature.
- ☐ **Thermistor** = thermally sensitive resistor made of sintered metal-oxide ceramic; 10 kΩ at 25 °C is the most common.
- ☐ **NTC** — resistance *decreases* with temperature; β-equation $R_T = R_0 \exp[\beta(1/T - 1/T_0)]$.
- ☐ **PTC** — resistance *increases* above switching temperature; used in resettable fuses.
- ☐ **CTR** — abrupt resistance jump; used in over-temperature protection.
- ☐ **TCR (α) of NTC** = $-\beta/T^2$ → typically −3% to −6% per °C, **10×** more sensitive than a Pt RTD.
- ☐ **Self-heating caution** — limit thermistor current to < 1 mA to avoid corrupting the measurement.
- ☐ **Steinhart–Hart equation** — three-coefficient model used for high-accuracy thermistor linearization.
- ☐ **Key biomedical uses** — clinical thermometry (T-type TC), incubators (NTC), PCR (Peltier), hyperthermia probes (TC arrays).
- ☐ **Always convert °C to K** before using thermistor formulas.
- ☐ **EMF is independent of wire length** (Law 3) — only materials and temperatures matter.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Magnetic Transducers: Hall effect transducers, LVDT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBMT404 - BIOSENSORS & TRANSDUCERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Magnetic Transducers: Hall effect transducers, LVDT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Hall Effect Transducer

### Formal Academic Definition
A **Hall Effect Transducer** is a solid-state, active magnetic sensing device that converts a magnetic field intensity (B) into a measurable transverse output voltage — known as the **Hall Voltage (V_H)** — based on the principle of the **Hall Effect**, discovered by **Edwin Hall in 1879**. When a current-carrying semiconductor or conductor is placed in a perpendicular magnetic field, the charge carriers (electrons or holes) experience a Lorentz force that deflects them sideways, creating a potential difference across the width of the material in a direction perpendicular to both the current and the magnetic field.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PBBMT404 / Module 3):**
> Hall Effect Transducers are classified under **Magnetic Transducers**. Students must know (i) the principle, (ii) the Hall coefficient, (iii) the expression for Hall voltage, (iv) applications in displacement/current/position sensing, and (v) why semiconductors (not metals) are preferred for the active element.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a wide, straight water canal carrying water (the **electric current**, I) flowing due north. Now imagine a strong wind blowing from the east toward the west (the **magnetic field**, B). The wind pushes the water sideways — toward the south bank — and a height difference builds up between the two banks. That height difference is exactly the **Hall Voltage**. The water's flow is the current, the wind is the magnetic field, and the height difference is what the voltmeter reads.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants Referenced in KTU Boards:**
> - Magnetic constant (permeability of free space): $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}\ \text{H/m}$
> - Elementary charge: $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$
> - Free electron mass: $m_e = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}\ \text{kg}$

---

## 1.2 Linear Variable Differential Transformer (LVDT)

### Formal Academic Definition
The **Linear Variable Differential Transformer (LVDT)** is a passive, inductive, electromechanical displacement transducer that converts linear mechanical displacement into a differential AC voltage output. Structurally, it consists of a **single primary winding** and **two identical secondary windings** wound symmetrically on a cylindrical former, with a movable soft-iron core (the armature) sliding coaxially inside. When the primary is energized with a sinusoidal AC excitation, the two secondaries produce EMFs that vary in opposing phase and magnitude as a function of core position.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PBBMT404 / Module 3):**
> For LVDT, students must master: (i) construction with labelled diagram, (ii) working principle for null, displacement-left, and displacement-right positions, (iii) phasor analysis and differential output, (iv) output characteristics curve, and (v) advantages like infinite resolution, frictionless operation, and high sensitivity.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of an LVDT as a **musical "balance scale" using invisible strings**. There is one input string (the primary) you pluck with AC electricity. Two output strings (the secondaries) sit side by side. A small iron weight (the core) slides up and down. If the weight sits exactly in the middle, both output strings sing equally loud — and because they are wired in *opposite phase*, the loudspeaker stays silent (null position). Push the core up — the top string sings louder, the bottom fades, and the speaker hums. Push the core down — the situation reverses. The **loudness difference** is proportional to the **displacement**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Differential"?** Because the output is the **arithmetic difference** $(E_{S1} - E_{S2})$ of two secondary EMFs. The word "differential" denotes this subtraction, which automatically cancels common-mode noise, temperature drifts, and excitation variations — a key reason LVDT is so accurate in industrial and biomedical instrumentation.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hall Effect — Structured Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Physical Setup
- A thin **rectangular semiconductor strip** of length $L$, width $w$, and thickness $t$ is considered.
- A **control current** $I$ is passed along the length $L$ (the $x$-axis).
- A **magnetic field** $B$ is applied perpendicular to the strip (the $z$-axis).
- The Hall voltage $V_H$ develops across the width $w$ (the $y$-axis) — this is the **transverse direction**.

### 2.1.2 Why Does the Voltage Appear? (The "Why" Behind Each Step)
1. **Charges move** with drift velocity $v_d$ due to the applied electric field from current $I$.
2. The **Lorentz force** $\vec{F} = q(\vec{v_d} \times \vec{B})$ pushes the moving carriers to one side of the strip.
3. Charge accumulation on one side creates a **transverse electric field** $E_H$ (the Hall field).
4. In **steady state**, the electric force $qE_H$ exactly balances the magnetic Lorentz force $q v_d B$ — meaning no further sideways deflection occurs.
5. The measurable potential difference across the width is the **Hall Voltage** $V_H = E_H \cdot w$.

### 2.1.3 Carrier Concentration & Sign Determination
- The **sign of $V_H$** reveals whether the dominant carriers are electrons (n-type, negative $V_H$) or holes (p-type, positive $V_H$).
- This makes the Hall effect one of the most powerful diagnostic tools in solid-state physics.

### 2.1.4 Why Semiconductors (Not Metals)?
- For the same current, the Hall voltage is **inversely proportional to the carrier density $n$**.
- Metals have $n \approx 10^{28}\ \text{m}^{-3}$ (very high), giving tiny $V_H$.
- Semiconductors have $n \approx 10^{21}$ to $10^{25}\ \text{m}^{-3}$ (much lower), giving **measurable, large $V_H$** — typically in millivolts, easy to amplify.

---

## 2.2 LVDT — Structured Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Construction Components
| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Primary winding ($N_P$)** | Energized with sinusoidal AC excitation $e_p = E_p \sin(\omega t)$ |
| **Secondary 1 ($N_{S1}$)** | Wound in series-opposition with Secondary 2 |
| **Secondary 2 ($N_{S2}$)** | Wound identically but reverse-phased to Secondary 1 |
| **Movable core** | Soft-iron/nickel-iron armature, slides linearly inside the former |
| **Former (bobbin)** | Non-magnetic cylindrical support on which windings sit |
| **Shielding** | Mu-metal casing for EMI rejection in industrial environments |

### 2.2.2 Working Principle (Three Operating Conditions)

**Condition 1 — NULL Position (Core at Geometric Center)**
- Core is symmetrically placed.
- Mutual inductance with $N_{S1}$ equals that with $N_{S2}$.
- $E_{S1} = E_{S2}$ in magnitude, but $180°$ out of phase (wound in series-opposition).
- **Net differential output $E_o = E_{S1} - E_{S2} = 0$**.

**Condition 2 — Core Displaced Toward Secondary 1 (Upward)**
- Flux linkage with $N_{S1}$ increases $\Rightarrow E_{S1}$ increases.
- Flux linkage with $N_{S2}$ decreases $\Rightarrow E_{S2}$ decreases.
- $E_{S1} > E_{S2}$ $\Rightarrow$ **Output is in phase with $E_{S1}$**, magnitude proportional to displacement $x$.

**Condition 3 — Core Displaced Toward Secondary 2 (Downward)**
- The reverse of Condition 2: $E_{S2} > E_{S1}$.
- **Output is in phase with $E_{S2}$ (i.e., $180°$ opposite to Condition 2)**.

> [!NOTE]
> The **phase of the output voltage** therefore encodes the **direction of displacement**, while the **magnitude of the output voltage** encodes the **amount of displacement** — a beautiful analog encoding scheme.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Critical for Board Exams)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hall Voltage (general) | $V_H = \dfrac{I \cdot B}{n \cdot q \cdot t}$ | $V_H$ in volts, $I$ in A, $B$ in T, $t$ in m, $n$ in m$^{-3}$ |
| 2 | Hall Voltage (using Hall Coefficient) | $V_H = \dfrac{R_H \cdot I \cdot B}{t}$ | $R_H = \dfrac{1}{nq}$ in m$^3$/C |
| 3 | Hall Coefficient | $R_H = \dfrac{1}{n \cdot q} = \dfrac{V_H \cdot t}{I \cdot B}$ | Sign of $R_H$ indicates carrier type |
| 4 | Hall Electric Field | $E_H = v_d \cdot B$ | Transverse field, V/m |
| 5 | Carrier Drift Velocity | $v_d = \dfrac{I}{n \cdot q \cdot A}$ | $A = w \cdot t$ is the cross-sectional area |
| 6 | Carrier Concentration | $n = \dfrac{I \cdot B}{q \cdot t \cdot V_H}$ | Important for Hall measurement experiments |
| 7 | Hall Effect Mobility | $\mu_H = \sigma \cdot \vert R_H \vert$ | $\sigma$ is electrical conductivity, $\mu_H$ in m$^2$/(V·s) |
| 8 | LVDT Primary Excitation | $e_p = E_p \sin(\omega t)$ | $E_p$ is peak primary voltage |
| 9 | LVDT Secondary EMF (S1) | $e_{S1} = E_1 \sin(\omega t)$ | $E_1 \propto$ mutual inductance with S1 |
| 10 | LVDT Secondary EMF (S2) | $e_{S2} = E_2 \sin(\omega t + 180°)$ | Reverse phase due to series-opposition winding |
| 11 | LVDT Net Differential Output | $E_o = e_{S1} - e_{S2} = (E_1 - E_2)\sin(\omega t)$ | Zero at null position |
| 12 | LVDT Sensitivity | $S = \dfrac{\Delta E_o}{\Delta x}$ | V/m — slope of linear region of $E_o$ vs $x$ curve |
| 13 | Mutual Inductance (LVDT) | $M \propto \dfrac{N_P N_S \mu_0 \mu_r A}{\ell}$ | Depends on core position via $\mu_r$ and overlap area |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Valuation Trap:** Students often write $V_H = \dfrac{IB}{nqt}$ and forget that $t$ is the **thickness along the magnetic field direction** (the $z$-axis), not the width $w$. Misidentifying the geometric dimension costs full marks in derivation questions.

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Hall transducers** are used in **brushless DC motor commutation**, **smartphone proximity sensors**, **automotive crankshaft position sensing**, **keyboard switches**, **current sensing clamps (Allegro ICs)**, and **magnetic levitation trains**.
- **LVDTs** are the gold standard in **aerospace flight-control surface feedback**, **hydraulic ram position feedback in CNC machines**, **nuclear reactor coolant valve monitoring**, **civil engineering extensometers**, and **biomedical devices** like muscle displacement probes and intra-ocular lens actuators.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Hall Voltage $V_H$

### Step 1: Define the Physical Geometry
Consider a rectangular semiconductor slab:
- Length along $x$-axis: $L$
- Width along $y$-axis: $w$
- Thickness along $z$-axis: $t$

The **current density** $\vec{J}$ flows along $+x$ direction, and the **magnetic field** $\vec{B}$ is applied along $+z$ direction (perpendicular to current).

### Step 2: Express Current Density
By definition of current density:

$$
J = n \cdot q \cdot v_d
$$

where $n$ is the carrier concentration, $q$ is the carrier charge, and $v_d$ is the drift velocity.

### Step 3: Force Balance in Steady State
Each moving carrier experiences a magnetic Lorentz force perpendicular to both $\vec{v_d}$ and $\vec{B}$. The cross product $\vec{v_d} \times \vec{B}$ deflects carriers toward the $+y$ (or $-y$) face, where they accumulate. The accumulated surface charge generates a transverse **Hall electric field** $E_H$ along $y$.

In steady state, the **electric force balances the magnetic force**:

$$
q E_H = q v_d B
$$

The $q$ cancels:

$$
E_H = v_d \cdot B
$$

### Step 4: Convert Drift Velocity to Current
The current $I$ flowing through the cross-section $A = w \cdot t$ is:

$$
I = J \cdot A = n q v_d \cdot w t
$$

Solving for drift velocity:

$$
v_d = \frac{I}{n q w t}
$$

### Step 5: Substitute Back into Hall Field
Inserting $v_d$ into $E_H$:

$$
E_H = \frac{I}{n q w t} \cdot B = \frac{I B}{n q w t}
$$

### Step 6: Convert Hall Field to Hall Voltage
The Hall voltage is the electric field integrated across the width $w$:

$$
V_H = E_H \cdot w
$$

Therefore:

$$
V_H = \frac{I B}{n q w t} \cdot w
$$

The width $w$ cancels (a key simplification that shows $V_H$ is **independent of width**):

$$
V_H = \frac{I \cdot B}{n \cdot q \cdot t}
$$

### Step 7: Introduce the Hall Coefficient
Define the **Hall Coefficient**:

$$
R_H = \frac{1}{n q}
$$

Then the compact form is:

$$
V_H = \frac{R_H \cdot I \cdot B}{t}
$$

### Step 8: Solve for Carrier Concentration
Rearranging for $n$ (a common KTU numerical question):

$$
n = \frac{I \cdot B}{q \cdot t \cdot V_H}
$$

This single formula is enough to characterize any Hall sample experimentally.

> [!NOTE]
> **Dimensional Check (SI):**
> $[n] = \dfrac{\text{A} \cdot \text{T}}{\text{C} \cdot \text{m} \cdot \text{V}} = \dfrac{\text{A} \cdot \text{kg/(A·s}^2)}{\text{A·s} \cdot \text{m} \cdot \text{kg·m}^2/(\text{A·s}^3)}$
> After cancellation: $[n] = \text{m}^{-3}$ ✓

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of LVDT Output Characteristics

### Step 1: Define the Mutual Inductances
Let $M_1$ = mutual inductance between primary and secondary 1.
Let $M_2$ = mutual inductance between primary and secondary 2.

The mutual inductances are **functions of core position $x$**:

$$
M_1(x) = M_0 - k x \quad \text{and} \quad M_2(x) = M_0 + k x
$$

where $M_0$ is the mutual inductance at the null (center) position, and $k$ is a sensitivity constant (H/m).

> **Why this linear model?** Near the null position, the change in flux linkage with core displacement is approximately linear. This is precisely the operating region the LVDT is designed to exploit.

### Step 2: Write the Induced Secondary EMFs
By Faraday's law, the EMF induced in a secondary is proportional to the rate of change of current in the primary, scaled by mutual inductance:

$$
e_{S1} = -M_1(x) \frac{di_p}{dt}
$$

The primary current itself is sinusoidal due to AC excitation:

$$
i_p = I_p \sin(\omega t) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{di_p}{dt} = \omega I_p \cos(\omega t)
$$

So:

$$
e_{S1} = -M_1(x) \, \omega I_p \cos(\omega t)
$$

Let $K = \omega I_p$ (a constant for fixed excitation frequency). Then:

$$
e_{S1} = -K \, M_1(x) \cos(\omega t)
$$

Similarly:

$$
e_{S2} = -K \, M_2(x) \cos(\omega t)
$$

### Step 3: Apply the Series-Opposition Winding
Because the two secondaries are wound in **opposite sense** and connected in series-opposition, the **net output is their difference**:

$$
E_o = e_{S1} - e_{S2}
$$

Substituting:

$$
E_o = -K \, M_1(x) \cos(\omega t) - \big( -K \, M_2(x) \cos(\omega t) \big)
$$

$$
E_o = -K \cos(\omega t) \big[ M_1(x) - M_2(x) \big]
$$

### Step 4: Substitute the Position-Dependent Inductances

$$
E_o = -K \cos(\omega t) \big[ (M_0 - kx) - (M_0 + kx) \big]
$$

$$
E_o = -K \cos(\omega t) \big[ -2 k x \big]
$$

$$
E_o = 2 k K x \cos(\omega t)
$$

Define the **sensitivity constant** $S = 2 k K = 2 k \omega I_p$ (in V/m):

$$
E_o = S \cdot x \cdot \cos(\omega t)
$$

### Step 5: Verify the Three Operating Conditions

| Condition | Core Position $x$ | $E_o$ | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Null** | $x = 0$ | $E_o = 0$ | Perfect balance |
| **Displaced right** ($+x$) | $x > 0$ | $E_o > 0$, phase $0°$ | $E_{S1} > E_{S2}$ |
| **Displaced left** ($-x$) | $x < 0$ | $E_o < 0$, phase $180°$ | $E_{S2} > E_{S1}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Result:** $E_o = S \cdot x \cdot \cos(\omega t)$ — output is **linear in displacement $x$**, with the **sign of $x$** encoded as a **$180°$ phase flip**, and the magnitude scaling with sensitivity $S$ which itself scales with excitation frequency $\omega$ and primary current $I_p$.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Hall Effect vs. LVDT Comparison

| Parameter | Hall Effect Transducer | LVDT |
|---|---|---|
| **Transduction type** | Active (no external excitation needed for sensing, but current $I$ must be supplied) | Passive (requires external AC excitation) |
| **Output signal** | DC or low-frequency Hall voltage | AC differential voltage |
| **Principle** | Lorentz force on moving charge carriers | Electromagnetic induction (Faraday's law) |
| **Primary input measured** | Magnetic flux density $B$ (and indirectly, current or position) | Linear displacement $x$ |
| **Sensitivity depends on** | Carrier concentration $n$, thickness $t$, control current $I$ | Excitation frequency $\omega$, primary current $I_p$, mutual inductance gradient $k$ |
| **Linearity** | Moderate (best in thin, uniform samples) | Excellent in central range (typically $\pm 5$ mm of full scale) |
| **Frequency response** | DC to several hundred kHz | Limited by primary inductance (typically up to a few kHz) |
| **Temperature stability** | Poor (carrier density $n$ varies with $T$) — needs temperature compensation | Excellent (both secondaries drift together) |
| **Typical application** | Magnetic field sensing, current clamps, BLDC commutation | Precision position/displacement feedback |
| **Resolution** | Limited by offset voltage and noise | **Infinite** (no friction, no mechanical detents) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the Hall Effect. Why are semiconductors preferred over conductors for fabricating Hall Effect sensors?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Hall Effect** is the phenomenon of generation of a transverse electromotive force (the **Hall Voltage, $V_H$**) across a current-carrying conductor or semiconductor when it is placed in a magnetic field perpendicular to the direction of current flow. Mathematically, the Hall voltage is expressed as:

$$
V_H = \frac{R_H \cdot I \cdot B}{t} = \frac{I B}{n q t}
$$

**Why semiconductors are preferred:**
1. Semiconductors have a **much lower carrier concentration** ($n \approx 10^{21}$ to $10^{25}\ \text{m}^{-3}$) compared to metals ($n \approx 10^{28}\ \text{m}^{-3}$). Since $V_H \propto 1/n$, semiconductors produce a **larger, easily measurable Hall voltage** (typically millivolts) for the same current and field.
2. The **sign of the Hall coefficient** in semiconductors clearly indicates whether the majority carriers are electrons (n-type) or holes (p-type), aiding material characterization.
3. Semiconductors have a **higher Hall coefficient magnitude** (e.g., $R_H$ for Si $\approx 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^3$/C, while for Cu it is $\approx -10^{-11}\ \text{m}^3$/C — a difference of 7 orders of magnitude).

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution (as per KTU 2024 scheme):** Definition: 1.5 marks | Justification with reasoning: 1.5 marks.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is an LVDT? List any four advantages of LVDT.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Linear Variable Differential Transformer (LVDT)** is a passive inductive transducer that converts linear mechanical displacement into a differential AC output voltage. It consists of a single primary winding and two secondary windings placed symmetrically on a cylindrical former, with a movable soft-iron core sliding inside.

**Four advantages of LVDT:**

1. **Infinite resolution** — Since the core does not make contact with the windings and there is no friction or mechanical detent, the output is a continuous, smooth function of displacement.
2. **High sensitivity** — Produces a usable output for displacements as small as a few micrometres.
3. **Excellent linearity** — The central operating region exhibits a near-perfectly linear $E_o$ vs. $x$ relationship.
4. **Low hysteresis** — Absence of friction means the output for increasing $x$ and decreasing $x$ is virtually identical.
5. *(Bonus — for extra credit)*: **Frictionless operation**, **ruggedness**, **insensitivity to temperature variations** (both secondaries drift together), and **no electrical noise from contact resistance**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution (as per KTU 2024 scheme):** Definition: 1 mark | Listing four advantages: 2 marks (0.5 mark each).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the Hall voltage in a semiconductor strip and define the Hall coefficient. (7 marks)

**(b)** A Hall effect sample of thickness $t = 0.5\ \text{mm}$ is carrying a current of $I = 2\ \text{A}$ in a magnetic field of $B = 1.2\ \text{T}$. The measured Hall voltage is $V_H = 50\ \text{mV}$. Calculate: (i) the Hall coefficient, (ii) the carrier concentration, and (iii) the carrier drift velocity. (7 marks)

---

### **Model Solution to Question A (a):**

The complete derivation is already presented in **Section 3.1** above. Students must reproduce the following key steps in the answer booklet for full marks:

**Step 1 — Define Current Density:**

$$
J = n q v_d
$$

**Step 2 — Force Balance (Lorentz force = Hall electric force):**

$$
q E_H = q v_d B \quad \Rightarrow \quad E_H = v_d B
$$

**Step 3 — Express Drift Velocity in Terms of Current:**

$$
v_d = \frac{I}{n q w t}
$$

**Step 4 — Combine to Get Hall Field:**

$$
E_H = \frac{I B}{n q w t}
$$

**Step 5 — Hall Voltage:**

$$
V_H = E_H \cdot w = \frac{I B}{n q t}
$$

**Step 6 — Define the Hall Coefficient:**

$$
R_H = \frac{1}{n q} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_H = \frac{R_H I B}{t}
$$

**Definition of Hall Coefficient:** $R_H$ is the transverse electric field developed per unit current density per unit magnetic field, with units of $\text{m}^3/\text{C}$. Its sign reveals the type of majority charge carrier.

---

### **Model Solution to Question A (b):**

**Given:**
- Thickness: $t = 0.5\ \text{mm} = 0.5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}$
- Current: $I = 2\ \text{A}$
- Magnetic field: $B = 1.2\ \text{T}$
- Hall voltage: $V_H = 50\ \text{mV} = 50 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{V}$
- Elementary charge: $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$

---

**(i) Hall Coefficient $R_H$:**

From the Hall voltage formula:

$$
R_H = \frac{V_H \cdot t}{I \cdot B}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
R_H = \frac{(50 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (0.5 \times 10^{-3})}{2 \cdot 1.2}
$$

$$
R_H = \frac{25 \times 10^{-6}}{2.4}
$$

$$
R_H = 1.0417 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}^3/\text{C}
$$

---

**(ii) Carrier Concentration $n$:**

By definition $R_H = \dfrac{1}{n q}$:

$$
n = \frac{1}{R_H \cdot q} = \frac{1}{(1.0417 \times 10^{-5}) \cdot (1.602 \times 10^{-19})}
$$

$$
n = \frac{1}{1.669 \times 10^{-24}}
$$

$$
n \approx 5.99 \times 10^{23}\ \text{m}^{-3}
$$

This value is characteristic of a lightly doped **n-type semiconductor** such as germanium or silicon.

---

**(iii) Carrier Drift Velocity $v_d$:**

Using $E_H = v_d B$ and $V_H = E_H \cdot w$, we need the width $w$ — but we can also use the cross-sectional area relation. From $I = n q v_d A$ with $A = w t$:

$$
v_d = \frac{I}{n q w t}
$$

We compute the area $A$ from the current density definition: $A = \dfrac{I}{n q v_d}$ — circular. Let's instead use the direct $E_H = v_d B$ approach.

$$
E_H = \frac{V_H}{w}
$$

But width is not given. We can express $v_d$ directly from the Hall field:

$$
v_d = \frac{E_H}{B}
$$

To find $E_H$ from given data, we use:

$$
E_H = \frac{I B}{n q w t}
$$

However, since width is not specified, we use the **simpler direct relationship** $E_H = v_d B$ combined with $V_H = \dfrac{I B}{n q t}$ to eliminate unknowns.

Alternative: From the force balance, $E_H = v_d B$, and the Hall voltage is $V_H = E_H \cdot w$. So:

$$
v_d = \frac{E_H}{B} = \frac{V_H / w}{B}
$$

Without $w$ given, we provide the symbolic answer in terms of the unknowns — a common KTU convention. The examiner expects:

$$
v_d = \frac{I}{n q w t}
$$

Substituting $n q = 1/R_H$:

$$
v_d = \frac{I R_H}{w t} = \frac{2 \cdot (1.0417 \times 10^{-5})}{w \cdot (0.5 \times 10^{-3})}
$$

$$
v_d = \frac{2.083 \times 10^{-5}}{5 \times 10^{-4} \cdot w} = \frac{0.0417}{w}\ \text{m/s}
$$

**Note:** If a typical width of $w = 2\ \text{mm} = 2 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}$ is assumed, then:

$$
v_d = \frac{0.0417}{2 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 20.83\ \text{m/s}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution for Q-A (b):** (i) 2 marks | (ii) 2.5 marks | (iii) 2.5 marks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the construction and working principle of an LVDT with a neat labelled diagram. Discuss its three operating conditions: null, displaced-left, and displaced-right. (7 marks)

**(b)** An LVDT is excited by a $5\ \text{V (rms)}$, $1\ \text{kHz}$ AC source. The sensitivity is $S = 0.5\ \text{V/mm}$. Find the output voltage (rms) and phase of the output for: (i) core displacement of $+3\ \text{mm}$ from null, and (ii) core displacement of $-2\ \text{mm}$ from null. (7 marks)

---

### **Model Solution to Question B (a):**

**Construction (refer to Section 2.2.1):** A labelled diagram should show:
- A hollow cylindrical former (non-magnetic, e.g., ceramic or phenolic).
- One **primary winding** ($N_P$) wound at the **center** of the former.
- Two **secondary windings** ($N_{S1}$ and $N_{S2}$) wound on either side of the primary, both identical in turns and wire gauge.
- A **soft-iron core** (the armature) that slides coaxially inside the former.
- A **shielded casing** (often mu-metal) to prevent external EMI.
- Lead wires brought out to terminals.

**Working Principle (refer to Section 2.2.2):**

| Condition | Position of Core | $E_{S1}$ | $E_{S2}$ | Differential Output $E_o = E_{S1} - E_{S2}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Null (center)** | At geometric center | Equals $E_0$ | Equals $E_0$ | $E_o = 0$ |
| **Displaced right ($+x$)** | Closer to $N_{S1}$ | $E_0 + \Delta E$ | $E_0 - \Delta E$ | $E_o = +2\Delta E$, in phase with $E_{S1}$ |
| **Displaced left ($-x$)** | Closer to $N_{S2}$ | $E_0 - \Delta E$ | $E_0 + \Delta E$ | $E_o = -2\Delta E$, in phase with $E_{S2}$ ($180°$ flipped) |

**Key Explanation:**
- The series-opposition winding ensures the two secondaries are **$180°$ out of phase** at all times.
- Only their **differential magnitude** appears at the output terminals — common-mode signals (excitation noise, temperature drift) are cancelled.
- The **amplitude** of the differential output is proportional to the magnitude of core displacement, and the **phase** (relative to the excitation) encodes the direction.

---

### **Model Solution to Question B (b):**

**Given:**
- Excitation: $E_p = 5\ \text{V (rms)}$, $f = 1\ \text{kHz}$
- Sensitivity: $S = 0.5\ \text{V/mm}$

**Derived formula (from Section 3.2):** $E_o = S \cdot x$, where $x$ is the displacement (signed) from null.

---

**(i) Displacement $x = +3\ \text{mm}$:**

$$
E_o = 0.5\ \text{V/mm} \cdot 3\ \text{mm} = 1.5\ \text{V (rms)}
$$

**Phase:** In phase with $E_{S1}$ (taken as reference, i.e., $0°$).

---

**(ii) Displacement $x = -2\ \text{mm}$:**

$$
E_o = 0.5\ \text{V/mm} \cdot (-2\ \text{mm}) = -1.0\ \text{V (rms)}
$$

The negative sign indicates the output is **inverted in phase** — i.e., it is **$180°$ out of phase** with the reference secondary. The magnitude is $1.0\ \text{V (rms)}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution for Q-B (b):** (i) 3.5 marks (1.5 for magnitude + 1 for phase + 1 for reasoning) | (ii) 3.5 marks.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Hall Effect — Sign Convention:** Forgetting to include the **sign** of the Hall coefficient when the question mentions n-type vs. p-type semiconductor. **Penalty: 1 to 2 marks lost.**
> 2. **Hall Effect — Thickness Confusion:** Writing $V_H = \dfrac{IB}{nqw}$ (using $w$ instead of $t$). The dimension is **thickness $t$** along the **magnetic field direction**. **Penalty: 2 marks lost.**
> 3. **LVDT — Missing Phase Indication:** Writing only the magnitude and not specifying the phase ($0°$ or $180°$) for the output. The phase encodes **direction**, so omitting it loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **LVDT — Confusing "Series-Aiding" with "Series-Opposition":** Students sometimes incorrectly state the secondaries are in series-aiding. The convention is **series-opposition**, which gives the differential (subtractive) output.
> 5. **Unit Errors:** Reporting Hall coefficient in $\text{m}^3/\text{C}$ correctly is worth 1 mark in itself; writing it in $\text{C/m}^3$ is wrong by orders of magnitude and loses 1 mark.
> 6. **No Diagram in 7-mark Questions:** For Part (a) of Question A or B, a **neat, labelled diagram is mandatory** and carries 1.5 to 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hall Effect Transducer — Key Takeaways:**
> - **Principle:** Lorentz force on charge carriers → transverse Hall voltage.
> - **Formula:** $V_H = \dfrac{I B}{n q t} = \dfrac{R_H I B}{t}$ — memorize this **verbatim**.
> - **Hall Coefficient:** $R_H = \dfrac{1}{n q}$ — units: $\text{m}^3/\text{C}$ — sign reveals carrier type.
> - **Semiconductor advantage:** Low $n$ → large $V_H$ (measurable, typically millivolts).
> - **Applications:** Magnetic field measurement, current sensing, BLDC motor commutation, contactless position/proximity sensing, keyboard switches.
> - **Limitations:** Temperature dependence of $n$, offset voltage (residual $V_H$ even at $B = 0$), non-linearity in thick samples.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LVDT — Key Takeaways:**
> - **Construction:** 1 primary + 2 secondaries + movable soft-iron core + non-magnetic former.
> - **Winding convention:** Secondaries are connected in **series-opposition** (anti-phase) for differential output.
> - **Null position:** $E_{S1} = E_{S2}$ → differential output $E_o = 0$.
> - **Displaced position:** $E_o = S \cdot x \cdot \cos(\omega t)$ — magnitude $\propto |x|$, phase encodes sign of $x$.
> - **Sensitivity $S$:** Increases with excitation frequency $\omega$ and primary current $I_p$.
> - **Advantages:** Infinite resolution, frictionless, low hysteresis, excellent linearity, self-cancelling temperature drift.
> - **Disadvantages:** Requires AC excitation, limited dynamic response, bulky compared to semiconductor alternatives.
> - **Applications:** Aerospace flight controls, CNC machine tool position feedback, hydraulic ram position, biomedical displacement probes.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Memory Aid (Mnemonics):**
> - **Hall** = "**H**orizontal **A**ccumulation of **L**orentz **L**oad" → charges pile up on the **L**ateral sides.
> - **LVDT** = "**L**ittle **V**oltages **D**ance with the **T**ravel" → output voltage changes with core travel, encoding both magnitude and direction.

---
